title: The Beginning
content:
HARDWAR – also known as Hardware Warfare, a type of warfare fought by privately owned, mostly remotely operated armoured assets engaged in fast-moving, small-scale actions against the orthodox military, police, and security forces and, on occasion, against rival commercial forces, either independently or in conjunction with a larger corporate-military strategy.

The word ‘hardwar’ (the abbreviation of hardware warfare) originates from the Ethiopian-Yemeni conflict (2068-69) that kept hundreds of thousands of Yemeni soldiers and partisans opposed by an army of remotely-controlled weapons systems, locked in what amounted to an extended public advertising campaign. The international press community coined the catchy term ‘hardwar’ for a description of the nature of this conflict. Since late 2080 this term also covers autonomous and AI warfare assets.

Frustrated orthodox military commanders have consistently damned Hardwar assets as ‘smart-cans’, ‘RTS toys’, ‘FPS extensions’, or ‘R/C troopers’, among many others.

— Universal Encyclopedia, Canal 2225 Edition

title: Climate Change
content:
The turn of the 22nd century was a difficult time for humanity. It was a time of exploration of the Solar System and beyond, but also a time of a series of ecological disasters. It was a time of technological marvels and a time of near-extinction. It was a time of great social changes and a time of the fall of the old order around the globe. It was a transition time that shaped space-faring humanity.

Not for the first time in history, hubris and self-interest propelled an over-optimistic vision of the future. By the mid-21st century, it was already too late. Despite the increasingly frantic warnings of lobby groups, campaigners and economists, the pressures of over-exploited natural resources, environmental pollution, and population growth brought the world to its tipping point. And even past the point of no return, vested interests had wrapped the international community in a straitjacket of stockpiled wealth that placed the governments of the United States, much of South America, and most of Europe effectively at their beck and call. At the same time, the culture of consumption defied all efforts to turn it around.

Then the High Tide came. Scientists had warned about global warming decades earlier, but pro-ecological initiatives worldwide proved too little, too late. Ice at both poles started to melt, as did the Siberian permafrost. Released methane and reduction of the polar caps accelerated the greenhouse effect, causing more thawing and melting. Over 40% of Earth’s 10 billion population had lived within 100 km of the world’s coastlines and, though it took a couple of decades, sea levels saw an almost twenty-metre rise by 2090.

The consequences were catastrophic: New York, Seattle, Miami, Shanghai, Hong Kong, Mumbai, Venice, Amsterdam, Hamburg, Barcelona, Dubai, and Buenos Aires, amongst so many other cities, eventually had to be abandoned. Bangladesh became the Bengal Sea.

The environmental impact didn’t end with increased sea levels. The Gulfstream vanished, causing drastic changes in the weather of the western hemisphere. Monsoons were affected due to the quickly increased oceanic temperatures. Again, the effects were disastrous: powerful storms ruined what was left of the East Coast of the US and the Caribbean, and dust storms and droughts turned large parts of southern Asia and North America into deserts. Europe was scorched with heatwave after heatwave.

In this cascade of disasters, there were a few positive accents. The thawed Siberian permafrost became one of the most fertile lands on Earth, quickly becoming the new world’s granary. Sahara, Australia, and the Middle East experienced increased rainfalls followed by a large vegetation boom. Amazon forests flourished, as did the planet’s marine life. Yet, elsewhere, many species became extinct and, if not for extensive DNA databanks and storage facilities, would have been lost forever.

Earth’s population became stable at 8 billion in 2080, partially due to famine, drought, and plagues that came with climate change, partially due to a strict birth control policy imposed in regions most affected by shortages of food, but mostly due to the anarchy that followed in most endangered areas. A few million emigrated to Earth’s orbit and beyond, to the newly made colonies on the Moon and Mars.

The general public had little understanding of what was going on in the initial stages of this ecocalypse, kept in the embrace of agenda-operated media and comfortable consumerism. But it wasn’t long before they felt its effects. In a cruel, ironic twist, the general public turned on the environmental lobby whom they saw as having failed them. Activists were driven underground and to increasingly violent and desperate acts to try to save what they could of the global civilization.

By 2090, the climate stabilized thanks to atmospheric converters repurposed from Martian areoforming efforts.

title: Political Shifts
content:
The climate situation was not the only change of 21st century Earth. Politics changed as well. The globalization of the previous century was blooming, creating an interconnected, cosmopolitical society interacting through social media, worldwide free access to satellite-feed Earthnet, and green and affordable global transport. National allegiance became much less important than being a citizen of the world, though it wasn’t so in the beginning.

A new power started to rise on a global level. Massive corporations had started merging into multinational entities, less and less dependent on government policies while at the same time more influential. Multinationals became transnationals, scattered around the globe in quickly emerging ‘flag of convenience’ countries, or even outside the jurisdiction of any government, in Earth’s orbit or on the Moon and Mars. It was no longer about money, it was about power. Most governments suddenly became hostages of transnational lobbyists. The message was simple: give us what we want or we will take our business, assets, and specialists elsewhere. Given that in some countries single transnationals generated up to 30% of the GDP, no alternatives were possible.

Transnationals were not the only ones influencing policies worldwide. Consumption patterns changed, especially in the energy sector. The world’s dependency on fossil fuels and nuclear isotopes diminished, replaced by renewable energy, efficient and cheap hydrogen cells, and the wide-scale introduction of cold fusion reactors. Around the middle of the century, uranium, oil, gas, and coal practically vanished from the market, replaced by hydrogen power produced from abundant seawater in electrolytic plants and bioreactors, and by even cheaper bioplastics. This caused further consequences to international politics. Strategically important resource-rich countries from the 20th century fell behind in the race for planetary domination. The old status quo made room for the new.

True transnationals were in their infancy, yet hungry for power. While diminishing superpowers were busy carving out new zones of influence, they left the rest of the world to itself – just what transnationals had been waiting for. Large-scope operations started in the most promising regions of the world: social campaigns, large investments in education and facilities, ecological initiatives, employment, security… sponsored by transnationals with one goal: control of governments and, in effect, countries. Kenya and Venezuela were among the first to relinquish their ineffective democratic governments and replace them with corporate management. Kenya, managed by mostly European consortia, became a model project, a seed of the African Federation. ‘Sponsored’ by more aggressive and ruthless Northern American corporations, Venezuela turned into an economic disaster, exploited and abandoned. For a decade, a similar pattern repeated around the world with varying success. When corporate executives turned their attention on the superpowers, the era of corpo-colonialism began.

title: African Federation
content:
With the markets of the Far East effectively closed to non-Chinese companies, with Europe boiling from diverging forces, and with the USA going through a Second Civil War, a cabal of the world’s most powerful transnationals agreed to run a deficit for twenty years to turn Africa into their continent.

With the prospering corporate Kenya project as an example, they met little resistance. Investment began to flow away from Europe and North America towards the emerging economies of Africa. With rising sea levels affecting impoverished coastal regions and apparently limitless corporate capital flowing into undeveloped and unprotected markets, the face of Africa changed irrevocably.

The Democratic Republic of the Congo was, at this point, enjoying a rare window of political stability. With its central location, proximity to mineral resources, and its strategic location on the fabled Congo River, Kinshasa became second to Nairobi as the African Union’s most favoured corporate capital. It also became a refuge port for American corporations evacuating assets from the war-torn USA. Within a decade, 90% of Silicon Valley moved to Kinshasa.

By 2065, Kinshasa had transformed into a sprawling and luxurious park-city, with entertainment centres, ethno-museums, and ultramodern residential and office buildings springing up in glass and steel along the sparkling promenades. The political and corporate systems worked hand-in-hand; not only jobs but research, manufacturing, and creative arts poured from the hands of the corporations. The nation’s primary problems of food shortages, disease, and crime were solved. Unsurprisingly, as African living standards improved dramatically, the corporations enjoyed astonishing approval ratings.

Under the benign pressure of the corporations, the African Union gave birth to the smaller, but politically more potent, African Federation. The 2066 Federation Act legalized the mercenary companies that had transformed from amoral militias into slick corporate partners, working with the transnationals to protect people and property. This provided the basis for the creation of a new wave of modern armies, financed from the pockets of companies, not states.

African Federation affiliated companies are the largest shareholders in the Kilimanjaro Space Elevator.

title: Cartel America
content:
With dwindling US interference in foreign affairs, the power vacuum was quickly filled with new apex predators. Cartels eventually took reign over extended Central America, from Mexico to Ecuador and Brazil. Today they are a distant echo of former drug lords and henchmen. Technically dictatorships born in coups, assassinations, and bloody guerrilla wars, most cartel-controlled areas are regions of stability, peace and prosperity. While border skirmishes remain common, a bunch of loose coalitions have recently been fortified by inter-cartel marriages, with pacts of mutual support and truces.

Cartel families became a sort of royalty of the 21st century, with an almost feudal structure of governorship and all that comes with it. Private armies are now recruited from loyal familias in exchange for profits, nepotism is common, and society, while free, is visibly segregated into those who own and those who don’t. Even the Catholic Church is still strong in the area.

Ecology and sustainability play a big part in Cartel America dealings. Cartels diversified from their usual drug production and distribution. They invited metanationals to open facilities on their lands and also started a few of their own, often investing loans from the Bank of Vatican. They are famed for breakthrough biotech, with cutting edge medicine, designer drugs, cybernetics and bodymods, security systems and infotech. Hackers coming from Cartel lands are sought-after all over the world.

title: China
content:
In the People’s Republic of China, central government and nationalization of the main sectors of industry had started way back in the 20th century, giving China a solid foundation from which to become one of the new national mega-corporations.

In the second half of the 21st century, after the collapse of the European Union and the United States, and with the Russian Federation taken over by The Syndicate, China was considered the only true national superpower left.

While highly successful, China was not without its problems. The rapid development of state-of-the-art manufacturing technologies and automation created many social problems as this greatly reduced the need for the most iconic element of China’s success – Chinese workers. China was forced to become the first true welfare state, with guaranteed benefits to every citizen, and where work and advanced education became rare privileges.

Children became a privileged luxury as well. By 2080, China’s population was reduced to a manageable 750 million and became stable.

Aside from basic income and free healthcare, every citizen of China now receives basic education and is scrupulously surveyed for future potential. Only the best are allowed to enter higher tiers of state-sponsored education, to become engineers, scientists, educators, and political elites. The remaining masses have few options to escape dull existence on welfare, the main ones being military service, entertainment, and colonization.

Despite the official image of state-controlled paradise, welfare society is full of black markets, with services and goods bartered between those who want more from life than food stamps. Gambling, drugs, prostitution, illegal fights, uncensored networking, even unlicensed street food are bartered in the darkest corners of Chinese mega-cities. Traditional Chinese secret societies are back in full bloom. Triads and tongs are flourishing, controlling the underworld, silently applauded by officials who rarely order punitive actions against citizens tainted by the black market. Triads act as an unofficial police force guarding the darkest and dirtiest layers of society.

China remains the largest manufacturer of goods on Earth and its colonies. It is also a leader in automation, employing ‘slave’ AIs in almost all aspects of life. While scientific progress is still behind the African Federation or European Consortium, Chinese specialists and scientists are sought after in every facility, making them valuable export commodities.

Foreign specialists are welcomed in Chinese mega-cities, enjoying western liberties in so-called Laowai Sectors, but, in general, foreign specialists cannot be promoted to key positions in most state-owned companies.

China is the major player in orbital settlements and factories.

title: European Consortium
content:
Europe has changed a lot since the beginning of the 21st century. Balanced between particular national interests and the common good, conservatism and liberalism, sooner or later it was doomed to shatter without a common goal.

Along came the Consortium of native corporations, led by Nova, to bring new purpose. Space colonization became a mantra, new joint effort breakthroughs in technology opening another era of conquest and trade for the Old Continent. Following the infamous path of Alexander the Great, the Roman Empire, Venetian Republic, Hanseatic League, and the East India Company, Europe reached for the stars with new hope. It was the first to mine asteroids parked on stable orbits around Earth, providing unimaginable quantities of previously scarce materials and resources. They had the first orbital and lunar colonies, with Mars settlements soon following. In a short time, the Consortium became so rich that it easily bought out the debts of all EU countries, becoming de facto owner of European countries, theoretically ending the continent’s democratic system.

This caused great uproar among EU citizens. Many radical factions emerged, used all forms of resistance, from artistic happenings, through online activity, marches, protests, and riots, ending in local uprisings as well as hacker and terrorist attacks. The peak of this activity came in 2057, when one of the radical groups, the National Anti-Globalization Front, detonated a nuclear device in the port of Marseilles, destroying a slowly sinking, though luckily mostly evacuated city.

Ironically, this single event helped the Consortium in taking total control of the façade European government, forcing it to dissolve and be replaced by Corporate Council under short-lived martial law prerogatives.

To everyone’s surprise, a new system was quickly established, every adult citizen receiving an equal share in Consortium’s holdings, becoming a voting member of the board. Anyone who wanted could trade their share on the open market, or return it to the Council officials in exchange for a ticket and place in one of the quickly growing extraterrestrial colonies. The European Consortium is the largest investor in the Martian colonisation effort.

title: Middle East
content:
When petrochemicals became obsolete, there was no easy way out for the Middle East. From Morocco to Afghanistan, from Syria to Ethiopia, anarchy became a new standard. Refugees flooded central Africa, Europe, and Central Asia.

Israel, abandoned by its western allies, became a literal fortress under constant siege, fighting for survival.

Today the Middle East is considered a lawless warzone full of shifting rogue states. Quite a few companies keep their foothold here, mostly black sites outside of any serious jurisdiction. It is also a great proving ground for new Hardwar technologies.

title: North America
content:
Canada has transformed greatly over recent decades. Climate impacted it heavily, changing coastlines and increasing temperatures. Farming became even more prosperous. Canada’s population increased five-fold during the 21st century, flooded with immigrants from the collapsing United States, Middle East, and Asia. Improving climate conditions allowed rapid settlement of large masses of people. New, corporate-sponsored cities emerged in areas north of 55° latitude, now enjoying moderate continental weather. Canada became a mosaic of corporate holdings, similar to the USA one century earlier.

The United States, once a world-shaking superpower, did not survive the 21st century, reaching economic collapse around the middle of the century. Coastline cities were flooded, agriculture was ruined by designer plagues and droughts, and the fossil fuels upon which the States heavily relied suffered a slow death. When the largest stock market bubble burst, the country was brought to its knees. Social unrest and heavy polarization of the population added riots and anarchy to the mix. Wild, lobbied pro-corporate policies finished off what was left, causing the whole country to follow the path of Detroit.

US Dollar inflation reached the point of no return, ending in WTO pulling the plug, declaring the USA bankrupt in 2057. The collapse of the government came shortly after. Free from government supervision, a few states successfully went for independence and built new local coalitions. No longer united, many former US states struggled to survive alone. Fourteen coalitions took place in the once single nation. California split into two republics, each with its own experimental form of governorship. Washington and Oregon merged into single militaristic dictatorship entities, bordering to the east with Mormon autonomy. Arizona became an independent Navajo territory.

East from Arizona and Mormon lands lies Dustland, a desolate area destroyed by droughts and sandstorms, with excess radioactive fallout from the Wolf Creek Incident, ending in seven states turned into radioactive wastelands of Chernobyl magnitude. Both Dakotas merged into Great Dakota, wedged between the Dustlands and Great Plains Federation. Great Lakes Megalopolis, also known as Rustlands, is east from there. Southward from GPF lies a coalition of Texarkana states, formed from Texas, Arkansas, and Louisiana. Free Republican states are wedged between Appalachia and Rustlands. North from Appalachia lies Northeast Union, south from it lies the Atlantic Coast Republic. What remains of Florida after the flood is now an independent, lawless pirate archipelago. Alaska sold itself to North Star Corp, Hawaii and other Pacific territories joined Oceania, and Puerto Rico became a base for a very peculiar cartel formed by employees and operatives of the dissolved CIA.

The USA disappeared from the political map of the Earth and became a hotbed of petty border conflicts. The only serious resource from the current USA is a spare workforce for high-risk jobs. American colonists willing to settle on the Moon, Mars, or other outworld colonies are a common sight, as are ‘soft asset’ mercenaries serving in many corporate securities cutting costs on hardware.

title: South Asia (Indian Subcontinent)
content:
The area south of the Himalayas was not as lucky as China. With climate change, monsoons changed as well. Bangladesh became 90% flooded, turning into the Bengal Sea. Over 100 million refugees sought asylum in Pakistan and India, adding to the problems both countries already had with droughts, pollution, and insufficient food supply. Aside from catastrophic weather, famine and plagues tormented the whole subcontinent for two decades, with almost no outside help. Hundreds of millions of people died in those dark times.

Pakistan underwent a painful religious civil war ignited by oppressed Bangladeshi refugees. The country collapsed and shattered into smaller warring states. Northern states of India, once the most populous, are now desolate wastelands, buffering India from Chinese Tibet. Most of the coastline was destroyed by record-breaking monsoons, bringing tsunami and high tides.

India overcame the crisis by turning to African and European corporatism, creating its own version of it. Each separate state was taken under the sponsorship of a willing multinational, giving them wide freedom in establishing local laws. India became one large amalgam of flag-of-convenience states.

title: Russian Syndicate
content:
When the governments of the EU and the US collapsed, Russia continued the evolution begun after the fall of the Soviet Union. By the mid-century, Russian Federation government evolved into the so-called Syndicate, a cabal of its most influential oligarchs. At the time, the whole government was a giant nepotistic, oligarchic, mafia-like run state, with the Federal Assembly and key functions of government filled almost exclusively by representatives of various national industries.

Syndicate is currently Earth’s largest supplier of energy. From cold fusion reactors through Helium-3 lunar refineries, hydrogen cells, and bioreactors, up to conventional solar and wind farms. Other sources of profit include still-available fossil fuel deposits and methane excavation in thawing Siberia.

The second big business is agricultural production, quickly expanding over thawed permafrost areas of Siberia. Orbital, Lunar, and Martian colonies are heavily dependent on the Syndicate supply chain.

Syndicate brought ‘recycling’ to a whole new level. Since the shortage of strategic materials like uranium and plutonium, large parts of the industry are salvage operations, concentrating on searching and stripping old and abandoned military bases, shut-down power plants, research complexes, and nuclear missile silos all over the world.

Russian Syndicate affiliates are leading in the colonization of the Moon.

title: West Pacific Rim & Oceania
content:
In the first half of the 21st century, Japan and Australia reformed their coalition, seeking dominance in the West Pacific Rim. Threatened by rising ocean levels, the coalition quickly grew to include New Zealand, Indonesia, South Korea, as well as Taiwan, Hong Kong, and Singapore. Soon after, most of Oceania joined the ranks, creating the West Pacific Rim.

A multitude of Oceania’s islands vanished under the sea, as did Hong Kong and a few other low-elevation pieces of land. Refugees fled to Australia and New Zealand, forever changing them.

Simultaneously with ecological disaster, the late 20th century concept of ‘imperial corporationism’ gained popularity in Japan and South Korea. Keiretsu and chaebols merged into powerful zaibatsu, quickly establishing their presence along the West Pacific Rim. Zaibatsu became crucial in financing help to sinking Oceania.

Australia established the New Hong Kong territory for refugees from Southeast Asia. It soon became a powerful ally, providing a massive business influx, balancing northern zaibatsu influence.

Pacific nations underwent massive changes due to climate disasters. Many former islanders became ocean nomads, living in floating arcologies and even undersea habitats, becoming true masters of the ocean. Underwater mining, algae farming, hydroponics, even whale farms became staples of Oceania.

title: Transnational Authority Council
content:
After conflict between United Lunar and Zarya Corp., transnationals discovered there was no authority to regulate their conduct. The UN was disbanded, the WTO was meaningless, even the World Court in the Hague had no authority to enforce its rulings.

Corporations quickly understood the need for independent authority to arbitrate and settle conflicts of interests between warring parties. In 2060, after months of negotiations between boards of the largest transnationals and governments, the Transnational Authority Council (TAC) was created, composed of corporate representatives. Each party agreed to subsidise it and adhere to its rulings, with a whole system of penalties, starting with fines and ending with banishment from markets.

title: Social Transformation
content:
Mankind adapted, but there had been dark days before evolution triumphed over revolution. The first stages were really violent. Slow but steady waves of refugees from sinking areas caused increasing tensions worldwide. Xenophobia, racism, segregation, and even pogroms and purges were commonplace in the first years of the flood. Communities were closing to strangers, immigrants were chased away or locked down in ghettos, while the old world order was collapsing.

With corporate rule came social changes. Multinational corporations by definition had to adapt to a multicultural work environment, so it was natural that they implement it in subsidiary countries. At the same time, in outworld colonies, old ways became meaningless, as people living there cared no more for old Earth. In most corporate-controlled regions, racial, gender, even political and religious differences were tossed aside in favour of a unified front against rival elements. It was also the largest Migration Period since the decline of the Western Roman Empire, mixing nations on an unprecedented scale, with great influence from transnationals and their hiring practices.

Facing disastrous climate change and new cultural shifts, most transnationals changed too. The image of greedy Wall Street sharks in suits was gone with Wall Street itself. With the devaluation of previously finite resources, merit became a new currency and reason for envy. People were no longer hired for their inheritance and connections, but for their knowledge and skills. Transnationals became too big for traditional management, and board decisions were replaced by AI strategies, leaving executives to fulfil them. With power so vast and resources so plentiful, or maybe due to long-term strategies of management AIs, transnationals lost interest in traditional forms of profit-making; now it was all about power. And, surprisingly, about the responsibility coming with it.

The largest responsibility was for environmental concerns. Most corporations embraced the idea of fixing the world and invested their resources in much-needed help. The first step was to minimise Tide damage. New agglomerations were built inland to accommodate refugees from coastal cities, providing housing, amenities, and work for those who needed it. European and African projects were quickly followed by those in Canada, Australia, Japan, and India, as well as some parts of Central America. China got its own megacities project and Russia moved much of its endangered population into thawing Siberia.

A merit-based system of values, as well as new frontier efforts, sparked enhanced evolution of the human species. While cybernetics and bio-modding had been in general use since the 2030s, the true revolution came with enhanced prenatal genome modification. The first generation of enhanced babies were born during the 2080s: stronger, smarter, and wiser, able to interact with AIs much better than regular homo sapiens.

A new civilization, or maybe even a new species was being born.

title: New Frontiers
content:
The late twenties and early thirties were all about a return to space. There were many firsts: the first private manned orbital launch, the first orbital hotel, the first orbital factory, the first commercial landing on the Moon, the first automated asteroid mine…

In 2029 the USA briefly returned to the Moon; a year later they were joined by China’s CNSA and the first permanent lunar habitats were built. A year later, ESA, heavily funded by Nova, launched the first successful manned mission to Mars, and three years later, ISRO/ESA missions established the first permanent base there. In 2040 RFSA/JAXA launched a manned mission to Venus, with the first research base established in Venus’s atmosphere two years later.

Private companies moved toward the Asteroid Belt and NEO objects, in a new gold rush.

By 2050, just before the breakdown of the old world order, Nova Consortium brought and successfully parked an asteroid in Earth’s orbit, creating a second Earth’s moon. 291 Alice, or just Alice, a Phobos-sized asteroid from the inner Asteroid Belt, became a source of rare elements that quickly destabilized markets around the globe and made Nova Consortium insanely rich. Alice Base, Nova’s orbital mining facility, quickly grew to be a significant frontier port and refuelling station for deep space missions.

The space race had changed during the collapse of superpowers. Earth became a dangerous place to be: millions of people wanted out, and they did what they could to get to orbit and beyond.

China focused mainly on the Near-Earth frontier, building space and lunar habitats, factories and cities. Golden Gate, their flagship project, took five years to finish, but when done, it housed over a million people and spawned a whole constellation of second-generation orbital cities.

The inhabitants of orbital cities found employment mainly in the ore processing factories, in transport between the colonies on the Moon and Mars, and in general service sector to such enormous populations.

Tourism and R&D played a significant role, while free professions such as journalists, artists, and performers turned spartan living conditions into a cultural experience to challenge that of struggling Earth.

Colonization of the Moon was rapidly accelerated by the practical application of Helium-3 in fusion reactors, allowing for clean and safe, high-output energy sources. Harvesting Helium-3 became a lucrative business, so transnationals all over the planet started to set up their own facilities. One of the strongest competitors was United Lunar, a private company owned by former employees of the disbanded NASA. Thanks to local know-how, United Lunar quickly gained the upper hand in the race for Helium-3-rich deposits, setting up bases in strategic locations. This caused friction with Syndicate-funded Zarya Corp., ending with the first extra-terrestrial armed conflict in 2059. This escalated quickly, and engulfed most of the Lunar settlements, concluding in the destruction of over 20% of lunar habitats and facilities.

Martian colonization began in 2039 with Mangala Outpost, quickly spawning nearby Concordia Base. The first transnational-driven waves of settlers were scientists and engineers tasked with the preparation of the planet for exploitation and mass colonization. But not everything went according to plan. With a high concentration of bright minds who had to rely more on their own ingenuity and vast planetary resources to survive, the new Martians quickly developed self-sustaining habitats across the globe. More difficult access to Mars than to the Moon limited direct supervision by their corporate overseers from Earth, allowing Martians more freedom than lunar colonists. Some groups even deserted their posts and started their own hidden settlements, unwilling to work for corporations.

In 2061 most of the Martian colonies declared independence from Terran rule. Sparse corporate security detachments on the surface were unable to effectively control the planet. A newly formed TAC was tasked with resolving the issue by all means necessary. The first shuttle carrying reinforcements was deflected with scatter missiles during its orbital entry and pushed toward the Asteroid Belt as a warning; the second was shot down with a commercial mass-driver used to send mined yield into orbit for pickup; the third shuttle successfully landed near Mangala Starport, only to face weaponized mining labots surrounding it. The crew were given an ultimatum – either return to orbit and contact their superiors to send negotiators or be destroyed and see Mars declare war against Earth.

Long negotiations between TAC and Martians ended with the Bill of Martian Independence. In exchange, Mars was obliged to accept a predefined annual quota of Earth refugees and allow for transnat business on the surface in ‘commercial zones’. Mars also accepted Nova assistance in aeroforming efforts, predicted to last at least a century.

Having seen the returns that Nova enjoyed from its investment in space exploitation, the African Federation-based transnationals have invested considerable resources in an effort to tether Alice to Earth by the Kiunga space elevator on Kilimanjaro. Started in 2070 with construction taking almost a decade, this freed mankind of Earth’s gravitational bind, a colonial effort accelerated rapidly.

Unlike Golden Gate, which had evolved far beyond its original plan, Kiunga facilities on Alice were designed from the outset as a place of residence for millions of people. The last basic module was connected only three years after the start of construction, but the expansion of the base, built with future growth in mind, took the next two decades. By the end of the century, it was home to several million people, by which time a halo of other orbital cities – Esperanza, New Africa, and Quetta – had been established.

The introduction of large quantities of Helium-3 introduced new types of fuel and engines, allowing for constant acceleration flight at 1G or more, allowing Mars to be reached in three days and Jupiter’s orbit within a week.

By the year 2099, mankind had permanent outposts on all four inner planets. Venus was home to a couple of hundred thousand people living in floating aerostat cities roughly 60 km above the surface. Mercury had a few small research outposts on its dark side.

Thousands of private prospectors populated the Asteroid Belt, which also became a base for large mining corporations, with Ceres, Vesta, and Juno serving as trading posts. The Jupiter moons, Io, Europa, Ganymede, and Callisto have been explored, and permanent outposts established with colonisation efforts considered for Europa.

Saturn’s moons have been explored in recent years, exclusively by Nova, with successful manned landings on Rhea, Titan, Tethys, and Iapetus. Titan’s permanent base is under construction. Iapetus is rumoured to be the site of an undercover research base.

Expeditions to outer planets and into the Oort Cloud are being planned.

In the meantime, groups of researchers are working on theoretical concepts of FTL drives, with some of them rumoured to be more than just theoretical concepts.

Finally, in recent years, astronomers are focusing on the Epsilon Eridani star and its companion, known as Aegir, which seems to emit unusual radio signals…

title: The Rise of Hardwars
content:
Hardwars happen, but what is the reason, incentive, and purpose behind them? Money, as usual, is the correct answer, but let us take a closer look at how they started.

The first thing to understand about Hardwar as a concept is that there are multiple levels of conflict awareness.

At the lowest, basic level it is just two warring forces, each focused on destroying the other. This is the level of awareness of the average civilian across the globe, watching the news. Above it lies a level of a cause – a slogan, idea, pledge, promise, even a coin – anything that convinces participants to risk their lives in actual combat, trying to eliminate other sides representing different ideas. This is what most rank-and-file soldiers are aware of.

Then we have the level of politics – actual reasons that leaders have to wage war: securing borders, expansion, eliminating competition are but a few of many reasons. This is the level of top commanders and politicians.

And until the second half of the 20th century that was the end of the pyramid of conflict awareness. However, the Cold War period added another layer to it – superpowers and their conflict sponsorship in smaller states. Delivering weapons and experts to ‘freedom fighters’ in exchange for future allegiance was a common practice, and helped in the destabilization of many underdeveloped countries, bending them to the will of superpowers. For obvious reasons, this was fully understood only by leaders of superpowers and their closest advisors.

At the dawn of the 21st century, superpowers became tools of lobbyists, more and more effectively controlling politicians. Sometimes even politicians were not aware of agendas their sponsors had.

Around the time of proxy wars, often fought to suppress strategic resources of competing countries, conflicts became a very powerful variable on stock markets around the world. And it actually outlived them when the United States, the largest bidder in such investments, collapsed. The irony is that, after the USA shattered into a dynamic mosaic of local militia alliances, it became a testbed for another level of financial speculation – Conflict Investments.

The idea was simple – to speculate on a chosen conflict, bidding on, or rather aiding a more promising side of the conflict. Those with money were aiding chosen sides, offering military help, equipment and supplies, in exchange for future concessions, contracts, or allegiance. Such investments often turned into bidding wars when both sides of the conflict had sponsors, pumping money into the chosen side to ensure domination, at least up to a certain point where projected profitability versus risk was acceptable. It is worth adding that many of those conflicts were actually ignited by sponsors of one of the sides.

Humanity accepted that wars were now waged for the empty coin, more and more detached from ideals and reasons. Death in such wars became a growing problem, especially in the aftermath of the Flood. Public opinion was saturated for decades with images of refugee camps, pandemics, starvation, poverty, and mass graves, and the public simply had enough. At the same time, insurance companies were raising their interest rates to cover and profit from increasing chaos. Flesh-made soldiers became a luxurious product for those who had money. Paradoxically those who had nothing could still field soft assets in abundance.

By that time it did not matter. Technological advancement allowed for a decrease of direct human presence on the battlefield. Remotely controlled robots and drones, unmanned combat vehicles, tactical AIs and, finally, the first cybernetic organisms were slowly pushing grunts into retirement. With mixed results at first.

Early remote hardware assets were powerful, but like any new technology, they were not without flaws. After an initial wave of successes, counter-strategies were developed to exploit their shortcomings. This was probably one of the most exciting and fastest military R&D races ever. Back doors in software were exploited. Behavioural algorithms were studied and driven into loopholes. Sensors were tricked and led astray.

Hardware developers responded with better security, improved algorithms, and even morally dubious experiments, like Exor’s Echo Delta project, employing biological brains built into the machine body. Each and every Echo Delta sooner or later became at best suicidal, at worst psychopathic, as it was in the case of Unit 20/9 and the infamous ‘Detroit Massacre’. While the whole Echo Delta project was scrapped, there are rumours of surviving prototypes out there, locked in hidden black labs. Similarly, early autonomous AIs were unreliable, breaking off formation, rampaging towards enemy lines just to freeze in critical moments, or else unresponsive to commands, lethargic, apathetic, or simply panicking in a firefight. This flaw, called Child Soldier Syndrome or Robo Shock took years of research to be eradicated.

Over time, those challenges were overcome, either by newer tech or by regress to earlier iterations. The most common solution is semi-autonomous control AI installed in the asset, with human handlers relying on commands remotely with the option to take over the asset in critical moments. Fully remote-controlled assets or autonomous robots are also found in Hardwar-era combat units.

With the advancement of hardware that didn’t know pain, fear, cold, or hunger, that could react with human instinct and artificial accuracy, and, most importantly, couldn’t sue for damages, atavistic flesh-and-bone soldiers were songs of the past.

And so a new breed of war, inhuman and cold, was born.

title: Mercenaries
content:
This is where independent contractors came to the stage. As with every investment, it was about minimizing costs and maximizing profits. Outsourcing soldiering to private contractors with a solid track record was a great way to achieve just that.

While mercenaries roamed the world since the invention of war, they flourished in the Hardwar era. With the gradual dissolvement of traditional nations and their armies, there were literally millions of professional jobless soldiers across the globe. Top brass, as usual, quickly found new, corporate masters to serve; some invested in paramilitary or tech startup companies, some became straight-up mercenaries. Many, too estranged to join civilian life and not good enough for corporate assets, ended up in crime sectors, becoming contract killers, mobsters, gun dealers, bandits, and pirates. Patriots of dying governments formed guerrilla forces, sometimes effective enough to hold territory and try to restore old ways, usually as dictators. Some even decided to implement their own utopias in controlled areas.

To add to the mix, the chaos and anarchy of the changing world was easily exploited by cunning ex-soldiers stealing and selling orphaned equipment on a scale much larger than after the fall of the Soviet Union or US withdrawal from Afghanistan – rifles and personal gear, through tanks and missile launchers, to aircraft carriers and nuclear submarines.

These events became a big problem for transnationals. Without any significant force to handle outlaws rapidly growing in strength, corporations decided to employ the ancient tactic of divide and conquer, using one asset they had in abundance – money. Targets were identified, bounties placed, and corporations could sit back and relax, watching a rapid worldwide cull of surplus ex-soldiers, fighting among themselves. If a mercenary team was slaughtered on the job, the price went up, and three more posses showed up to try again until one succeeded.

In less than a decade, most of the rogue elements were either eliminated, under control, or added to the employment roster of one of the new masters. The rest were forced into hiding in the most economically barren areas of the world. Bounty hunters that survived this time became rich enough to build their own financial empires, only to be hired again by transnationals for protection.
